policy_statement: By 2023, offer targeted technical assistance for BIPOC and low-income owners and tenants on incentives, rebates, and financing for electrification (BO.2-9).
mechanism_description: The city will begin offering targeted technical assistance and information on incentives, rebates, and financing options to help BIPOC and low-income owners and tenants adopt building electrification, reducing fossil fuel use in buildings and associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Program support/technical assistance → ↑ electrification adoption → ↓ on-site fossil fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: The city provides targeted technical assistance and guidance on incentives, rebates, and financing to overcome informational/transaction barriers for low-income and BIPOC owners/tenants, increasing installation of electrified building equipment.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Electrified end-uses replace combustion-based space/water heating and cooking, shifting energy demand from on-site gas/oil combustion to grid electricity.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower direct (Scope 1) building emissions and typically lower total lifecycle emissions depending on grid carbon intensity, producing net GHG reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced direct fossil fuel combustion in buildings (and associated GHG emissions) via higher adoption of electrified end-uses enabled by technical assistance.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Enabling support increases electrification adoption, which primarily reduces fossil fuel combustion emissions in buildings.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: The core intended and largest climate effect is direct GHG reduction, not primarily energy efficiency (though it may occur) or adaptation/resilience.  
3) Edge cases applied: If electrification increases electricity use and the grid is very carbon-intensive, net GHG benefits could be smaller; however the mechanism’s dominant intent/effect remains emissions reduction through fuel switching. Equity targeting affects distributional outcomes, not the primary climate category.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism—technical assistance aimed at accelerating building electrification—operates through the same fuel-switching pathway that reduces building emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferral: Whether the main quantified benefit is Scope 1 vs. total GHG reductions, co-pollutant reductions, or peak-load impacts depends on local grid mix and program design and should be assigned at the individual policy-evidence level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential ambiguity with “Resource Efficiency” if a program is framed mainly as reducing energy use; however, electrification technical assistance is dominantly a fuel-switching mitigation mechanism. Net GHG impact depends on marginal grid emissions and potential induced load/peak effects.
key_indicators: “begin offering targeted technical assistance”; “including information about incentives, rebates, and public and private financing options”; “BIPOC and low-income owners and tenants”
co_benefits: Equity and affordability benefits (improved access to programs/financing for priority communities); potential indoor air quality and health improvements from electrification.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as an incentive-program if the policy itself created/provided rebates/financing; here it only provides guidance/navigation to existing incentives and financing, so information-disclosure is the best fit.
